content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Prasanna W. Rasiah 
Assistant City Attorney 
1017 Middlefield Road 
Redwood City, CA 94063 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-17-256 
Dear Mr. Rasiah: 
January 17, 2018 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Redwood City Councilmember 
Diane Howard regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section I 090. Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (/11 re 
Oglesby ( 1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71 ), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and 
accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should 
contact us for additional advice. 
Your letter requests further advice on behalf of Councilmember Howard concerning 
decisions that could affect her interest in Sequoia Main Street, LLC. In our prior letter, (Smith 
Advice Letter No. 1-17-094) we addressed decisions essentially the same as those posed in your 
request. With the exception of the decisions relating to the architectural style and pedestrian access 
proposals, you have provided no new facts that would alter our prior analysis. 
QUESTIONS 
1. May Councilmember Howard participate in a decision on an amendment to remove 
contemporary architecture as an option in the North of Marshall District? 
2. May Councilmember Howard participate in a decision to close Middlefield Road between 
Winslow Street and Broadway, as well as Broadway between Theatre Way and Hamilton, to 
vehicular access, thereby creating pedestrian-friendly commercial areas? 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


CONCLUSION 
File No. A-17-256 
Page No. 2 
I & 2. Yes. Neither decision would have a material financial effect on the value of 
Councilmember Howard's interest in the LLC, therefore, the Act does not prohibit her from 
participating in these decisions. 
FACTS 
Together with her spouse, Councilmember Howard has a 4.34358% interest in Sequoia 
Main Street, LLC ("LLC"). The LLC owns the historic Sequoia Hotel at 800 Main Street, which is 
located within the City's DTPP area. The hotel consists of fifty-four rooms that have shared 
bathroom facilities. The hotel functions as a single-room occupancy hotel and all rooms are rented 
on a weekly or monthly basis at rates lower than those in typical business and tourist hotels. In 
addition to the hotel rooms, the building contains ground floor retail tenants who lease space from 
the LLC. The retail tenants currently include an antique store, a butcher shop, a liquor/grocery store, 
a nail salon, a book store, and a tobacco shop. The hotel provides no automobile or bicycle parking. 
As minority owners in the LLC, Councilmember Howard and her husband in the past have 
received a periodic distribution from the LLC derived from operating profits from the real estate 
and hotel operations, which is comprised of business income, rental income, and interest income. 
Councilmember Howard's investment in the LLC is valued at $2,000 or more. However, 
during the past 12 months, she has not received any distributions from the LLC . .Her last 
distribution from the property occurred on August 15, 2015. 
Development Under Redwood City's Downtown Precise Plan 
Development in Redwood City's downtown area is governed by the DTPP, which was 
adopted on January 24, 2011 and amended on September 10, 2012, July 22, 2013, September 12, 
2016, and November 28, 2016. A DTPP Program environmental impact report was certi fled by the 
City Council on January 24, 2011. 
The DTPP establishes land use, development, and urban design regulations for the 183-acre 
DTPP area. Pursuant to Section 52.5 of the Redwood City Zoning Ordinance, amendments to the 
DTPP require approval of the City Council after a public hearing. 
Architectural Style 
The DTPP has established six architectural "Character Zones" in the downtown area. Within 
these zones, six different "Character Types" are allowed in varying combinations depending on the 
nature of the zone and the preferences of the community. The zones include: Historic Downtown, 
Stambaugh-Heller Transition, Courthouse Square, El Camino Corridor, Mezesville Transition, and 
North of Marshall District. Contemporary architecture is only permitted in the North of Marshall 
District. The City Council may consider an amendmen to remove contemporary architecture as an 
option in this zone. The Sequoia Hotel is located in the Historic Downtown zone where 
contemporary architecture is not an available option. 


Pedestrian Access and Amenities 
File No. A-17-256 
Page No. 3 
The City Council may consider closing Middlefield Road between Winslow Street and 
Broadway (this stretch of road is known as "Theatre Way") as well as Broadway between Theatre 
Way and Hamilton to vehicular access (other than emergency vehicles and occasional deliveries), 
thereby creating pedestrian-friendly commercial areas. The Sequoia Hotel is located approximately 
two blocks away from both of these roadways. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. 
(Section 87103.) Section 87103 defines the interests from which a conflict of interest may arise. 
These interests are: 
1. Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment2 worth 
$2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a).) 
2. Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth 
$2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b).) 
An "indirect" investment or interest in real property is defined as "any investment or interest 
owned by the spouse or dependent child of a public official, by an agent on behalf of a public 
official, or by a business entity or trust in which the official, the official's agents, spouse, and 
dependent children own directly, indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent interest or greater." 
(Section 87103.) Because the councilmember has less than 10-percent ownership interest in the 
LLC, the investments fd property owned by the LLC are not interests of the councilmember. 
3. Any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial lending institution made in 
the regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status, 
aggregating $500 or more in value provided or promised to, received by, the public official within 
12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. (Section 87103(c).) 
"Income" is defined to include any community property interest in the income of a spouse 
and a pro rata share of the income of any business engity or trust in which the official ( or his or her 
spouse) owns directly, indirectly, or beneficially, a 10-percent or greater interest. (Section 
82030(a).) However, because the Councilmember has less than IO-percent ownership interest in the 
LLC, the clients of the company are not sources of income to Councilmember Howard. (Section 
82030(a).) 
4. Any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, 
employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103( d).) 
2 "Indirect investment" is defined to include any investment or interest owned by the official's spouse. (Section 
87103.) 


File No. A-17-256 
Page No. 4 
5. Any donor of, or any intennediary or agent for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating 
[$470] or more in value provided to, received by, or promised to the public official within 12 
months prior to the time when the decision is made. (Section 87103( e ). ) 
6. Personal finances, meaning the financial effect of a governmental decision on the 
personal finances of a public official or his or her immediate family. Regulation 18702.5 sets forth 
the materiality standard applicable to a personal financial effect, and subdivision (c) of that 
regulation provides that if a decision also affects an official's interest in a business entity or real 
property, materiality is detennined pursuant to Regulation 18702.1, relating to business interests, or 
Regulation 18702.2, relating to real property interests, respectively. 
Councilmember Howard is a public official subject to the Act. Given that her investment in 
the LLC is valued at $2,000 or more, she has an interest in the LLC as a business entity. (Section 
87103.) She also has an interest in the LLC as a source of income assuming she receives a 
distribution that equals or exceeds $500 in the 12 months prior to a governmental decision. (Section 
87103(c).) Thus, she would have a conflict of interest in decision that will have a reasonably 
foreseeable and material financial effect on the LLC. 
Foreseeability 
Under the Act, an effect on an interest is presumed foreseeable if the interest is explicitly 
involved in the decision, and an interest is explicitly involved in the decision if the interest is a 
named party, or subject of, the decision. (Regulation 1870 l(a).) The LLC is not "explicitly 
involved" in either decision. Therefore, the effect of the decision on the LLC is reasonably 
foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or 
theoretical. (Regulation 18701(b).) 
Materiality: Business Eutity and B11si11ess Entity as So11rce of Income 
The standard to detennine materiality, for a business entity not explicitly involved in the 
decision inciuding a business entity as source of income, is set forth in Regulation 18702.l(b). 
Materiality is found if "a prudent person with sufficient infonnation would find it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision's financial effect would contribute to a change . . .  in the value of a 
privately-held business entity." (Regulation 18702.l(b).) 
Architectural Style 
A proposed amendment to remove contemporary architecture as an option in the North of 
Marshall District would not have a foreseeable and material financial effect on the value of the 
LLC. The Sequoia Hotel is not located within this district, and this amendment would not impact 
the hotel or the surrounding neighborhood, where contemporary architecture is currently not an 
available option. 
Pedestrian Access and Amenities 
The Sequoia Hotel is located approximately two blocks away from the sections of 
Middlefield Road and Broadway that may be closed to traffic. Although the Sequoia Hotel has 


File No. A-17-256 
Page No. 5 
several retail shops such as the liquor/grocery store, nail salon and tobacco shop that undoubtedly 
rely on walk-up business, the closure of two sections of roadway that are not in the immediate 
vicinity of the Sequoia Hotel would not add to pedestrian traffic around the hotel, and does not 
appear to have a foreseeable and material financial effect on the value of the LLC. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
ZWN:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Brian G. Lau 
Assistant General Counsel 
0·01,L._ 
Zachary W. Norton 
By: 
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

sections.question:
1. May Councilmember Howard participate in a decision on an amendment to remove 
contemporary architecture as an option in the North of Marshall District? 
2. May Councilmember Howard participate in a decision to close Middlefield Road between 
Winslow Street and Broadway, as well as Broadway between Theatre Way and Hamilton, to 
vehicular access, thereby creating pedestrian-friendly commercial areas?
sections.conclusion:
I & 2. Yes. Neither decision would have a material financial effect on the value of 
Councilmember Howard's interest in the LLC, therefore, the Act does not prohibit her from 
participating in these decisions.

sections.analysis:
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. 
(Section 87103.) Section 87103 defines the interests from which a conflict of interest may arise. 
These interests are: 
1. Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment2 worth 
$2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a).) 
2. Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth 
$2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b).) 
An "indirect" investment or interest in real property is defined as "any investment or interest 
owned by the spouse or dependent child of a public official, by an agent on behalf of a public 
official, or by a business entity or trust in which the official, the official's agents, spouse, and 
dependent children own directly, indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent interest or greater." 
(Section 87103.) Because the councilmember has less than 10-percent ownership interest in the 
LLC, the investments fd property owned by the LLC are not interests of the councilmember. 
3. Any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial lending institution made in

"Income" is defined to include any community property interest in the income of a spouse 
and a pro rata share of the income of any business engity or trust in which the official ( or his or her

4. Any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, 
employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103( d).) 
2 "Indirect investment" is defined to include any investment or interest owned by the official's spouse. (Section 
87103.)  
5. Any donor of, or any intennediary or agent for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating 
[$470] or more in value provided to, received by, or promised to the public official within 12

2. May Councilmember Howard participate in a decision to close Middlefield Road between 
Winslow Street and Broadway, as well as Broadway between Theatre Way and Hamilton, to 
vehicular access, thereby creating pedestrian-friendly commercial areas?

CONCLUSION: I & 2. Yes. Neither decision would have a material financial effect on the value of 
Councilmember Howard's interest in the LLC, therefore, the Act does not prohibit her from 
participating in these decisions.
